name: Lighthouse Cabo de Palos and Salinas de Marchamalo — gravel circular through the Regional Park of Calblanque
name_original: Faro Cabo de Palos y Salinas de Marchamalo — gravel circular por el Parque Regional de Calblanque
region: National Park  Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila
region_intro: Touring cycling around National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila offers routes through a diverse landscape. The region features coastal areas with salt flats and beaches, alongside more elevated terrain characterized by hills and rocky outcrops. This natural park provides varied conditions for touring cyclists, from flat coastal paths to routes with moderate ascents. The area is known for its natural beauty, including sand dunes and Mediterranean flora. Best touring cycling routes around National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila The most popular touring cycling route is Lighthouse Cabo de Palos and Salinas de Marchamalo — gravel circular through the Regional Park of Calblanque, a 14.5 miles (23.3 km) trail that takes 1 hour 45 minutes to complete. This route features gravel paths through coastal landscapes and salt flats. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is The Pleistocene Jewel Mural – Sand Dune Trail loop from Rincón de San Ginés, a difficult 27.5 miles (44.2 km) path. This trail navigates through sand dunes and offers views of the natural park. Local touring cyclists also love the La Manga of the Mar Menor – Playa Honda Beach loop from Rincón de San Ginés, a 8.1 miles (13.0 km) trail leading through coastal areas and along beaches, often completed in about 55 minutes. Touring cycling around National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila is defined by coastal paths, sand dunes, and varied hilly terrain. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to different ability levels. The routes in National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 170 reviews. More than 2000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila?
A: There are over 85 touring cycling routes in the National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila region. These routes cater to various skill levels, offering a mix of easy, moderate, and challenging options.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Calblanque National Park?
A: Yes, the region offers a good selection of easy routes, with over 25 options available. A good starting point is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33791637" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Manga of the Mar Menor – Playa Honda Beach loop from Rincón de San Ginés&lt;/a&gt;, which is 8.1 miles (13.0 km) long and features coastal paths and beaches.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in this National Park?
A: The terrain varies significantly, ranging from flat coastal paths and salt flats to routes with moderate ascents through sand dunes and more elevated, rocky outcrops. You'll encounter both paved and gravel sections, offering a diverse cycling experience.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Calblanque National Park?
A: Yes, many routes in the park are circular. For example, the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1016726337/lighthouse-cabo-de-palos-and-salinas-de-marchamalo-gravel-circular-through-the-regional-park-of-calblanque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lighthouse Cabo de Palos and Salinas de Marchamalo — gravel circular through the Regional Park of Calblanque&lt;/a&gt; is a 14.5-mile (23.3 km) loop that takes you through coastal landscapes and salt flats.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33827239" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Pleistocene Jewel Mural – Sand Dune Trail loop from Rincón de San Ginés&lt;/a&gt; offer a difficult 27.5-mile (44.2 km) path navigating through sand dunes with significant elevation changes. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/21864599" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa Honda Beach – The Mar Menor loop from Playa Honda&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 35.6 miles (57.2 km).

Q: What is the best season for touring cycling in National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila?
A: The best seasons for touring cycling in this region are typically spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these periods, temperatures are milder, and the weather is generally more stable, making for more comfortable rides. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cooler and wetter.

Q: Are there scenic viewpoints or natural attractions along the cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in natural beauty. You can find stunning views at &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7048648" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de Cenizas&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1054778" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Fuente&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7211523" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas and Peña del Águila Regional Park&lt;/a&gt; itself offers diverse landscapes, including sand dunes and pristine beaches like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/199520" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calblanque Beach&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I spot wildlife while cycling in the National Park?
A: Yes, the National Park Calblanque, Monte de las Cenizas y Peña del Águila is a protected area known for its biodiversity. Cyclists often have the chance to spot various bird species, especially around the salt flats and coastal areas. Keep an eye out for flamingos and other migratory birds, particularly during spring and autumn.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling options in the area?
A: Many of the easier, flatter coastal paths are suitable for families. These routes often have less traffic and provide opportunities to enjoy the scenery at a relaxed pace. Consider routes that stick to the less challenging sections of the park, especially those near beaches or designated recreational areas.

Q: Where can I find parking for accessing the touring cycling routes?
A: Parking is generally available at key access points to the National Park and near popular beaches or towns like Rincón de San Ginés and Playa Honda, which serve as common starting points for many routes. Always check local signage for specific parking regulations and availability, especially during peak seasons.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in this region?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 170 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, from the stunning coastal views and pristine beaches to the unique sand dunes and the challenging climbs in the more elevated sections. The variety of routes catering to all skill levels is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks to explore along the routes?
A: Yes, the region has some interesting historical sites. You can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4460935" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Chapa Battery and Portmán Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt;, which offer a glimpse into the area's past and provide panoramic views of the coastline. These landmarks can be incorporated into longer cycling tours.
tips: One of the beautiful viewpoints on this adventurous hiking trail.

The beautiful lighthouse of Cabo de Palos began operating in 1865. Originally its shape and construction was different, it was demolished a few years before and rebuilt on the foundations, finishing in 1865. Today you can get closer and as usual in lighthouses, the views they are simply wonderful!

This protected space has a rich history regarding the production of salt ("white gold") that marked an era in the area of Cartagena and the Mar Menor.
At the end of the 19th century, the salt industry was established, which over the years lost momentum due to low productivity and became a mainly tourist area with a real estate boom.
Today it is a beautiful place to visit and tour where you can still appreciate some constructions from the salt era and also spot native birds.
